code: # Base R data frames: auto-detects "base" orders_df <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   amount = c(100, 250, 75),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers_df <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Diana"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  result_base <- left_join_spy(orders_df, customers_df, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(result_base) #> [1] "data.frame" # Tibbles: auto-detects "dplyr" orders_tbl <- dplyr::tibble(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   amount = c(100, 250, 75) )  customers_tbl <- dplyr::tibble(   id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Diana") )  result_dplyr <- left_join_spy(orders_tbl, customers_tbl, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(result_dplyr) #> [1] "tbl_df"     "tbl"        "data.frame" # data.tables: auto-detects "data.table" orders_dt <- data.table::data.table(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   amount = c(100, 250, 75) )  customers_dt <- data.table::data.table(   id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Diana") )  result_dt <- left_join_spy(orders_dt, customers_dt, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(result_dt) #> [1] "data.table" "data.frame" # data.table + tibble: data.table wins mixed_result <- left_join_spy(orders_dt, customers_tbl, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(mixed_result) #> [1] "data.table" "data.frame"

Another difference row ordering. Base R’s merge() sorts rows key column. dplyr preserves row order left table. data.table sorts key tables keyed, otherwise preserves insertion order. analytical workflows order matter, comparing output across backends tests reproducibility checks, sort result comparing. simplest rule: use project already depends . preference, base R safest default. performance matters, data.table. want tibble output tidyverse compatibility, dplyr.

code: sales <- data.frame(   product = c("Widget", "Gadget ", " Gizmo"),   units = c(10, 20, 30),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  inventory <- data.frame(   product = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   stock = c(100, 200, 300),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(sales, inventory, by = "product") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: product #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 1 #> Keys only in left: 2 #> Keys only in right: 2 #> Match rate (left): "33.3%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left column 'product' has 2 value(s) with leading/trailing whitespace #> ℹ 2 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'Gadget ' ~ 'Gadget', ' Gizmo' ~ 'Gizmo') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 1 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 5 sales_clean <- join_repair(sales, by = "product") #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) key_check(sales_clean, inventory, by = "product") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected shipments <- data.frame(   warehouse = c("East ", "West", "East "),   product   = c("Widget", "Gadget ", "Gizmo"),   shipped   = c(50, 80, 35),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  stock <- data.frame(   warehouse = c("East", "West", "East"),   product   = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   on_hand   = c(200, 150, 90),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(shipments, stock, by = c("warehouse", "product")) #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: warehouse, product #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 0 #> Keys only in left: 3 #> Keys only in right: 3 #> Match rate (left): "0%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left column 'product' has 1 value(s) with leading/trailing whitespace #> ℹ 1 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'Gadget ' ~ 'Gadget') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Per-Column Breakdown ── #>  #> warehouse: "50%" match rate (1/2) #> product: "66.7%" match rate (2/3) #> ℹ Lowest match rate: warehouse #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 0 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 6 shipments_clean <- join_repair(shipments, by = c("warehouse", "product")) #> ✔ Repaired 3 value(s) key_check(shipments_clean, stock, by = c("warehouse", "product")) #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

text: Databases often case-insensitive. R . pull tables two different systems, one might store "ABC" "abc". refer entity, R treats different keys. None keys match -. join_spy() picks : Repair sides common case: choose target case. "lower", "upper", "title" work. practice, "lower" safest default. Lowercase unambiguous (locale-dependent rules title case), database systems normalize lowercase internally. Upper case fine , title case introduces edge cases acronyms prepositions vary language. One subtlety: key column factor, case standardization changes level labels underlying integer codes. can produce factors whose levels look identical lowering originally pointed different groups. Convert character first (.character()) standardizing case factor columns; otherwise risk silently merging distinct factor levels.
code: sensors <- data.frame(   station = c("AWS-01", "aws-02", "Aws-03"),   temp = c(22.1, 18.4, 25.7),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  metadata <- data.frame(   station = c("aws-01", "AWS-02", "AWS-03"),   region = c("North", "South", "East"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) join_spy(sensors, metadata, by = "station") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: station #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 0 #> Keys only in left: 3 #> Keys only in right: 3 #> Match rate (left): "0%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! 3 key(s) would match if case-insensitive (e.g., 'AWS-01' vs 'aws-01') #> ℹ 5 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'AWS-01' ~ 'AWS-02', 'AWS-01' ~ 'AWS-03', 'aws-02' ~ 'aws-01') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 0 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 6 repaired <- join_repair(sensors, metadata, by = "station", standardize_case = "lower") #> ✔ Repaired 4 value(s) key_check(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "station") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

Two duplicates left times three right gives six rows key alone. real data can turn 10,000-row join million-row table. put concrete numbers : suppose left table 50,000 rows right table 30,000 rows, average 4 duplicates per key side. resulting join can exceed 100 million rows, consuming gigabytes memory taking minutes complete hardware handles original tables milliseconds. detect_cardinality() tells relationship type: production pipelines, m:m explosions dangerous beyond obvious memory cost. 50,000-row table joined 30,000-row table moderate duplication can silently produce tens millions rows. Downstream aggregations (sums, means, counts) operate inflated table return wrong numbers; results look plausible, nobody catches error business decision already made. code: items <- data.frame(   order_id = c(1, 1, 2, 2, 2),   item = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  payments <- data.frame(   order_id = c(1, 1, 2, 2),   method = c("Card", "Cash", "Card", "Wire"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  check_cartesian(items, payments, by = "order_id") #> ✔ No Cartesian product risk (expansion factor: 2x) detect_cardinality(items, payments, by = "order_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "m:m" #> Left duplicates: 2 key(s) #> Right duplicates: 2 key(s) join_strict(items, payments, by = "order_id", type = "left", expect = "1:m") #> Error: #> ! title: Common Join Problems
text: join goes wrong, work steps order. problems resolve first three. Step 1: Run join_spy(). always starting point. checks string quality, key overlap, cardinality, predicted row counts single call. large datasets (hundreds thousands rows ), full diagnostic scan can slow. Pass sample = 1000 run join_spy() random subset first; catches common problems seconds rather minutes. sampled report looks clean, run without sampling confirm. Step 2: Fix string issues. report flags whitespace, case, encoding, empty strings, run join_repair(). handles common class silent failures. Step 3: Check duplicates. predicted row count higher expected, inspect duplicates key_duplicates() decide whether aggregate deduplicate. Step 4: Enforce cardinality. keys clean, use join_strict() guarantee relationship expect. catches edge cases slip manual inspection. Step 5: Explain result. joined output still looks wrong, run join_explain() get breakdown happened: Step 6: Log audit trails. production workflows, want record join_spy() found repairs applied. Use set_log_file() top pipeline route subsequent reports log file automatically: especially useful debugging issues days weeks pipeline ran; log tells exactly key distributions looked like join time. pattern : diagnose strings first, structure, cardinality. String problems invisible common. Structural problems visible know look. find repeating workflow across multiple joins pipeline, consider using analyze_join_chain() diagnose entire sequence joins ; applies checks every step reports first problem enters chain.
code: report <- join_spy(x, y, by = "key_col") x_clean <- join_repair(x, by = "key_col") # or both sides: repaired <- join_repair(x, y, by = "key_col", standardize_case = "lower") key_duplicates(y, by = "key_col") result <- join_strict(x_clean, y_clean, by = "key_col",                       type = "left", expect = "1:1") result <- left_join_spy(x_clean, y_clean, by = "key_col") join_explain(result, x_clean, y_clean, by = "key_col") set_log_file("logs/join_diagnostics.log") # All join_spy() / join_explain() calls now append to this file

text: key_check() returns single logical: TRUE issues detected, FALSE otherwise. makes natural fit assertions. simplest pattern wraps stopifnot(): keys problems, script halts: warn = FALSE argument suppresses printed diagnostics. cron job CI pipeline, generally want script fail hard fast rather print warnings nobody reads. need control error message, use explicit /stop pattern instead: verbose gives custom error message can include table names, pipeline step, link documentation. choose tradeoff. common pattern chains assertion repair step. Run key_check() first; fails, repair re-check: use key_check() versus join_spy()? serve different roles. key_check() gate, binary pass/fail costs almost nothing run. join_spy() detailed diagnostic builds full report object match rates, expected row counts, categorized issues. production script, key_check() assertion run every execution; join_spy() pull assertion fails need understand . Calling join_spy() every run fine moderate-sized tables, million-row datasets overhead adds , key_check() leaner choice.
code: orders <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C01", "C02", "C03", "C04"),   amount = c(100, 200, 150, 300),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C01", "C02", "C03", "C04"),   region = c("East", "West", "East", "North"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # This passes -- keys are clean stopifnot(key_check(orders, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)) orders_dirty <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C01", "C02 ", "C03 ", "C04"),   amount = c(100, 200, 150, 300),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  stopifnot(key_check(orders_dirty, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)) #> Error: #> ! key_check(orders_dirty, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE) is not TRUE if (!key_check(orders_dirty, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)) {   stop("Key quality check failed for orders-customers join. ",        "Run join_spy() interactively for details.", call. = FALSE) } #> Error: #> ! Key quality check failed for orders-customers join. Run join_spy() interactively for details. ok <- key_check(orders_dirty, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)  if (!ok) {   repaired <- join_repair(     orders_dirty, customers,     by = "customer_id",     trim_whitespace = TRUE,     remove_invisible = TRUE   )   orders_clean <- repaired$x   customers_clean <- repaired$y    # Re-check after repair   stopifnot(key_check(orders_clean, customers_clean,                        by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)) } #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s)

text: *_join_spy() wrappers print diagnostic output default. output helpful working interactively; noise scheduled script. .quiet = TRUE argument suppresses printed output still computing storing diagnostic report internally. report still available fact via last_report(): separation execution inspection key pattern. join runs silently inside pipeline. Later (maybe validation step end script, maybe separate monitoring job) pull report check contents programmatically: gives diagnostic power join_spy() without cluttering standard output pipeline. report object persists memory next *_join_spy() call overwrites , race condition long script single-threaded (R scripts ). can build arbitrarily complex validation logic top report object. Check number issues, inspect specific issue types, compare expected row count threshold; whatever pipeline requires. report plain list, standard R subsetting works. Nothing validation requires interactive use; programmatic. One caveat: last_report() stores single recent report. script performs three joins sequence, third report survives. need retain reports earlier joins, either log (see next section) capture explicitly:
code: sensors <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("S01", "S02", "S03", "S04"),   location = c("Roof", "Basement", "Lobby", "Garage"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  readings <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("S01", "S02", "S03", "S05"),   temperature = c(22.1, 18.5, 21.0, 19.3),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Nothing printed result <- left_join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", .quiet = TRUE) rpt <- last_report() rpt$match_analysis$match_rate #> [1] 0.75 rpt <- last_report() if (rpt$match_analysis$match_rate < 0.95) {   warning(sprintf(     "Low match rate (%.1f%%) in sensor join -- check for missing sensor IDs",     rpt$match_analysis$match_rate * 100   )) } #> Warning: Low match rate (75.0%) in sensor join -- check for missing sensor IDs result1 <- left_join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", .quiet = TRUE) report1 <- last_report()  # ... later ... result2 <- inner_join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", .quiet = TRUE) report2 <- last_report()

title: Joins in Production
text: join one--one development can become many--many production upstream data changes. join_strict() prevents enforcing cardinality constraint. constraint violated, function throws error instead silently producing Cartesian product. workflow two phases. development, use detect_cardinality() understand actual relationship: result tells one--many relationship: product appears products can appear multiple times line_items. expected products reference table line items transactional. Now encode expectation production: someone accidentally loads products table duplicate product IDs (turning relationship many--many), script fails immediately: four cardinality levels correspond real data pattern: 1:1 means lookup table joined another lookup table. key appears exactly sides (country codes currency codes, employee IDs badge numbers). either side duplicates, something went wrong ETL. 1:m applies left table reference right table transactions measurements: products order line items, customers support tickets, stations hourly weather readings. m:1 mirror image: left table transactions right table serves lookup. Examples include sales records joined region table, survey responses joined demographic codebook. m:m indicates duplicates sides. almost always bug. legitimate use case generating pairwise combinations two attribute sets, rare enough requiring explicit expect = "m:m" acts speed bump. practice, "1:m" "m:1" cover vast majority production joins. Use detect_cardinality() development confirm relationship, hard-code expect value production script. constraint costs almost nothing runtime (checks duplicates key columns performing join) yet catches class upstream data quality problems otherwise propagate silently. just use check_cartesian() instead? two functions solve different problems. check_cartesian() warns Cartesian product explosion, situation key many duplicates sides join result grows quadratically. join_strict() enforces relationship contract. join can violate "1:1" constraint without triggering Cartesian explosion (one extra duplicate enough). Conversely, "m:m" join can produce massive Cartesian product join_strict() allow "m:m" loosest constraint. Use join_strict() know expected relationship. Use check_cartesian() additional guard expected relationship "m:m" want bound expansion factor.

Cardinality violation: expected 1:m but found m:m #>   Left duplicates: 1, Right duplicates: 2

Use .rds need complete report post-mortem analysis; use text JSON need human-readable machine-parseable record.

title: Joins in Production
text: scripts perform many joins, setting log file top cleaner calling log_report() join. set_log_file() enables automatic logging: every subsequent *_join_spy() call appends report specified file. pattern production script : set log file start, run joins, disable logging end (let R’s session cleanup handle ). log accumulates timestamped record every join, including match rates, issue counts, expected row counts. something goes wrong downstream, can trace back log see join introduced problem. Automatic logging triggers *_join_spy() wrappers. call join_strict() bare merge() directly, nothing logged. design: wrappers “instrumented” path, join_strict() lightweight guard build full report. want cardinality enforcement logging, run detect_cardinality() separate check, use left_join_spy() (whichever wrapper fits) actual join. complete production pattern end vignette demonstrates exactly approach. get_log_file() returns current log path (NULL logging disabled), handy conditional logic:
code: auto_log <- tempfile(fileext = ".log") set_log_file(auto_log, format = "text") #> ℹ Automatic logging enabled: C:/Temp\RtmpKCPMYC\file1d90018b05631.log  # These joins are automatically logged result1 <- left_join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", .quiet = TRUE) result2 <- inner_join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", .quiet = TRUE)  # Check what got logged cat(readLines(auto_log), sep = "\n") #>  #> Logged: 2026-03-31 09:32:44 #> ------------------------------------------------------------ #> Join Key: sensor_id #>  #> Left Table (x): #>   Rows: 4 #>   Unique keys: 4 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Right Table (y): #>   Rows: 4 #>   Unique keys: 4 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Match Analysis: #>   Keys in both: 3 #>   Keys only in left: 1 #>   Keys only in right: 1 #>   Match rate: 75% #>  #> Expected Rows: #>   inner_join: 3 #>   left_join: 4 #>   right_join: 4 #>   full_join: 5 #>  #> Issues Detected: 1 #>   near_match: 1 #> ============================================================ #>  #> Logged: 2026-03-31 09:32:44 #> ------------------------------------------------------------ #> Join Key: sensor_id #>  #> Left Table (x): #>   Rows: 4 #>   Unique keys: 4 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Right Table (y): #>   Rows: 4 #>   Unique keys: 4 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Match Analysis: #>   Keys in both: 3 #>   Keys only in left: 1 #>   Keys only in right: 1 #>   Match rate: 75% #>  #> Expected Rows: #>   inner_join: 3 #>   left_join: 4 #>   right_join: 4 #>   full_join: 5 #>  #> Issues Detected: 1 #>   near_match: 1 #> ============================================================  # Clean up set_log_file(NULL) #> ℹ Automatic logging disabled unlink(auto_log) # Only log if logging is configured if (!is.null(get_log_file())) {   message("Logging is active at: ", get_log_file()) }

title: Joins in Production
text: Running full diagnostics table ten million rows takes time. sample parameter join_spy() runs analysis random subset actual join (use *_join_spy() wrapper) still operates full data. sampled report approximate. Match rates duplicate counts estimated sample rather computed exactly. production monitoring, fine; want know whether match rate roughly 95% roughly 60%, whether 94.7% 95.1%. sample catches systemic problems (wrong key column, widespread encoding issues, massive duplicate explosion) fraction runtime. tradeoff: sampling can miss rare issues. 0.1% keys zero-width space, 5,000-row sample 10-million-row table might include . reason, run full diagnostics periodically (weekly, whenever upstream data source changes) use sampled diagnostics daily runs. layered approach gives speed executions thoroughness matters. large sample ? universal answer, sample 10,000 50,000 rows catches systemic issues. goal statistical significance; catching problems affect meaningful fraction keys. pipeline joins ten tables, sampling one 10,000 rows keeps total diagnostic overhead second even modest hardware. diagnostics always cheaper join , sample size rarely needs tuned aggressively.
code: # Simulate a large dataset set.seed(42) big_orders <- data.frame(   customer_id = sample(paste0("C", sprintf("%04d", 1:5000)), 50000, replace = TRUE),   amount = round(runif(50000, 10, 500), 2),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  big_customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = paste0("C", sprintf("%04d", 1:6000)),   region = sample(c("North", "South", "East", "West"), 6000, replace = TRUE),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Full analysis system.time(report_full <- join_spy(big_orders, big_customers, by = "customer_id")) #>    user  system elapsed  #>    0.14    0.03    0.18  # Sampled analysis system.time(report_sampled <- join_spy(big_orders, big_customers,                                         by = "customer_id", sample = 5000)) #>    user  system elapsed  #>    0.07    0.02    0.09

title: Joins in Production
text: sections introduced tool individually. wired together single script represents realistic production workflow. scenario: nightly job loads order customer data, validates keys, repairs needed, joins cardinality enforcement, logs everything audit. gate script serves different purpose. key_check() gate catches string-level problems (whitespace, encoding, case) attempts automatic repair. detect_cardinality() gate checks relationship two tables: orders customers many--one, customer table suddenly duplicates (common ETL failure), script halts producing Cartesian product. row count check final sanity guard: left join preserve rows left table, , something gone wrong gates catch. logging runs background throughout set_log_file() called top. Every *_join_spy() call appends timestamped report log file. script finishes, log contains complete record every join: checked, found, predicted row counts . audit trail pays debugging data quality issue days pipeline ran. pattern scales. Add tables, gates, joins, structure stays . Set logging , assert key quality join, enforce cardinality know expected relationship, check row counts . overhead minimal: key_check() detect_cardinality() O(n) number rows, join_strict() adds duplicate check top join . million-row tables, add sample = 50000 join_spy() calls diagnostics become bottleneck. alternative trust. Trust upstream data clean, trust ETL introduce duplicates, trust key columns still match. Trust works , breaks, cost always higher cost checks .
code: # ============================================================ # Nightly order enrichment pipeline # ============================================================  # --- Setup logging --- pipeline_log <- tempfile(fileext = ".log") set_log_file(pipeline_log, format = "text") #> ℹ Automatic logging enabled: C:/Temp\RtmpKCPMYC\file1d900356337fa.log  # --- Load data (simulated) --- orders <- data.frame(   order_id = 1:6,   customer_id = c("C001", "C002 ", "C003", "C003", "C004", "C005"),   amount = c(150, 230, 89, 410, 320, 175),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C001", "C002", "C003", "C004", "C005", "C006"),   name = c("Acme Corp", "Globex", "Initech", "Umbrella", "Soylent", "Wonka"),   tier = c("gold", "silver", "gold", "bronze", "silver", "gold"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # --- Gate 1: key quality assertion --- keys_ok <- key_check(orders, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)  if (!keys_ok) {   message("Key issues detected -- attempting repair")   repaired <- join_repair(     orders, customers,     by = "customer_id",     trim_whitespace = TRUE,     remove_invisible = TRUE   )   orders <- repaired$x   customers <- repaired$y } #> Key issues detected -- attempting repair #> ✔ Repaired 1 value(s)  # --- Gate 2: cardinality check --- card <- detect_cardinality(orders, customers, by = "customer_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "m:1" #> Left duplicates: 1 key(s) if (card == "m:m") {   set_log_file(NULL)   unlink(pipeline_log)   stop("Unexpected m:m cardinality in orders-customers join", call. = FALSE) }  # --- Join (with auto-logging via *_join_spy) --- enriched <- left_join_spy(orders, customers, by = "customer_id", .quiet = TRUE)  # --- Gate 3: row count sanity check --- # A left join should never lose rows from the left table if (nrow(enriched) < nrow(orders)) {   set_log_file(NULL)   unlink(pipeline_log)   stop("Row count decreased after left join -- possible data corruption",        call. = FALSE) }  # --- Output --- message(sprintf("Pipeline complete: %d enriched orders", nrow(enriched))) #> Pipeline complete: 6 enriched orders head(enriched) #>   customer_id order_id amount      name   tier #> 1        C001        1    150 Acme Corp   gold #> 2        C002        2    230    Globex silver #> 3        C003        3     89   Initech   gold #> 4        C003        4    410   Initech   gold #> 5        C004        5    320  Umbrella bronze #> 6        C005        6    175   Soylent silver  # --- Review the log --- if (file.exists(pipeline_log)) {   cat(readLines(pipeline_log), sep = "\n") } #>  #> Logged: 2026-03-31 09:32:44 #> ------------------------------------------------------------ #> Join Key: customer_id #>  #> Left Table (x): #>   Rows: 6 #>   Unique keys: 5 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Right Table (y): #>   Rows: 6 #>   Unique keys: 6 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Match Analysis: #>   Keys in both: 5 #>   Keys only in left: 0 #>   Keys only in right: 1 #>   Match rate: 100% #>  #> Expected Rows: #>   inner_join: 6 #>   left_join: 6 #>   right_join: 7 #>   full_join: 7 #>  #> Issues Detected: 1 #>   duplicates: 1 #> ============================================================  # --- Cleanup --- set_log_file(NULL) #> ℹ Automatic logging disabled unlink(pipeline_log)

title: Quick Start
text: Trailing leading whitespace single common reason keys fail match. column exported Excel read CSV often contain "London " alongside "London", R sees two distinct values. printed, report flags whitespace problems tells exactly values affected. match_analysis section shows match rate issues fixed; , "Paris " " Berlin" fail match clean counterparts, dropping match rate 100% 50%. Whitespace problems compound keys span multiple columns. Suppose join city district: Now London fails district column Paris fails city column. column checked independently; row needs key columns match, single trailing space one enough break join. quick pass/fail gate scripts, use key_check(): key_check() returns FALSE whitespace. warn = TRUE (default), also prints issues found. makes key_check() useful assertion automated pipelines: wrap stopifnot() script halts broken join silently drops rows. Unlike join_spy(), returns full report object, key_check() deliberately minimal (single logical value) slots cleanly conditional logic.
code: sales <- data.frame(   city = c("London", "Paris ", " Berlin", "Tokyo"),   revenue = c(500, 300, 450, 600),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  cities <- data.frame(   city = c("London", "Paris", "Berlin", "Tokyo", "Madrid"),   country = c("UK", "France", "Germany", "Japan", "Spain"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(sales, cities, by = "city") sales2 <- data.frame(   city = c("London", "Paris ", "Berlin"),   district = c("West ", "Central", " Mitte"),   revenue = c(500, 300, 450),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  districts <- data.frame(   city = c("London", "Paris", "Berlin"),   district = c("West", "Central", "Mitte"),   pop = c(200000, 350000, 180000),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(sales2, districts, by = c("city", "district")) key_check(sales, cities, by = "city") #> ! Key check found 1 issue(s): #> ✖ Left table column 'city' has whitespace issues (2 values)

title: Quick Start
text: Databases spreadsheets handle case differently. CRM might store "ACME" billing system stores "Acme", R treats distinct strings. case-mismatched pair listed output. "Initech" matches exactly; three lost standard join. Case conflicts particularly common merging data systems different conventions: CRMs uppercase everything, ERPs title-case, web forms accept freeform input. match_analysis section report shows potential match rate without case sensitivity, can gauge whether standardizing case recover lost matches.
code: invoices <- data.frame(   company = c("ACME", "globex", "Initech", "UMBRELLA"),   amount = c(1200, 800, 950, 1500),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  vendors <- data.frame(   company = c("Acme", "Globex", "Initech", "Umbrella"),   sector = c("Manufacturing", "Logistics", "Software", "Biotech"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(invoices, vendors, by = "company")

text: Encoding issues nastiest. Nothing visible debug. Two strings render identically console differ byte level: non-breaking space (\u00A0) versus regular space, Latin-1 accented character versus UTF-8 equivalent. zero-width space appended "SKU-003" invisible printed prevents join matching. joinspy flags encoding issues. common culprits include non-breaking spaces (\u00A0), byte-order marks, right--left marks creep PDF copy-paste web scraping. Standard R string functions (trimws(), nchar()) catch operate visible characters . identifying specific Unicode code points involved, joinspy saves considerable debugging time compared staring two strings look identical console.
code: # Simulate invisible character contamination raw_ids <- data.frame(   product_id = c("SKU-001", "SKU-002", paste0("SKU-003", "\u200B")),   batch = c("A", "B", "C"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  clean_ids <- data.frame(   product_id = c("SKU-001", "SKU-002", "SKU-003"),   warehouse = c("East", "West", "North"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(raw_ids, clean_ids, by = "product_id")

Zero matches four rows. Grouped problem type (whitespace, case, encoding), output lets decide address one. matters fix differs: whitespace safe trim automatically, case standardization requires choosing direction (upper, lower, title), encoding issues sometimes indicate deeper data pipeline problems trimming alone solve. grouped output lets prioritize; often fixing whitespace case recovers 90% lost matches, remaining encoding problems can handled case case.

"London" appears three times left table twice right, get six rows London inner join, often unintentionally. key_duplicates() returns offending rows along count: Use keep = "first" keep = "last" return first last occurrence per key. default keep = "" returns every row participates duplicate group. .n_duplicates column output tells group size, directly maps row multiplication factor: key .n_duplicates = 3 left table .n_duplicates = 2 right produce 6 rows key inner join.
code: transactions <- data.frame(   store_id = c("S1", "S1", "S2", "S3", "S3", "S3"),   day = c("Mon", "Tue", "Mon", "Mon", "Tue", "Wed"),   amount = c(100, 200, 150, 300, 250, 400),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  key_duplicates(transactions, by = "store_id") #>   store_id day amount .n_duplicates #> 1       S1 Mon    100             2 #> 2       S1 Tue    200             2 #> 4       S3 Mon    300             3 #> 5       S3 Tue    250             3 #> 6       S3 Wed    400             3

title: Quick Start
text: Always preview repairs applying . dry_run = TRUE option shows change without modifying data: dry run tells many values change direction, without touching original data frames. Review output carefully: shows values modified become. Review carefully case standardization, uppercasing key like "c-303" "C-303" correct, want verify tables agree target format committing.
code: messy_left <- data.frame(   id = c(" A-101", "B-202 ", "c-303", paste0("D-404", "\u200B")),   score = c(88, 92, 76, 95),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  messy_right <- data.frame(   id = c("A-101", "B-202", "C-303", "D-404"),   label = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma", "Delta"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_repair(messy_left, messy_right, by = "id", dry_run = TRUE) #>  #> ── Repair Preview (Dry Run) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Left table (x) ── #>  #> ℹ id: trimmed whitespace (2), removed invisible chars (1)

title: Quick Start
text: Drop dry_run (set FALSE) get back repaired data frames. pass x y, return value list : sides now clean, uppercase, whitespace-free keys. can pass repaired tables straight join. confirm repairs worked, run key_check() repaired tables: return TRUE warnings. Running key_check() repair good practice catches edge cases trimming case standardization enough (instance, two distinct keys collapse value uppercasing). One important caveat: join_repair() fixes mechanical string problems: whitespace, case, invisible characters, empty strings. fix typos, semantic mismatches, fundamentally different key formats. one table uses "USA" uses "United States", one stores product codes "P-001" uses "001", repair bridge gap. require manual recoding lookup table.
code: repaired <- join_repair(   messy_left, messy_right,   by = "id",   trim_whitespace = TRUE,   standardize_case = "upper",   remove_invisible = TRUE ) #> ✔ Repaired 4 value(s)  repaired$x$id #> [1] "A-101" "B-202" "C-303" "D-404" repaired$y$id #> [1] "A-101" "B-202" "C-303" "D-404" key_check(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "id") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

R’s merge() match NAs default (neither dplyr), either table contains NA keys, predicted row count may overestimate actual result. report warns NAs present key columns can account . summary() returns information compact data frame:
code: orders <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P1", "P2", "P3", "P4"),   quantity = c(10, 5, 20, 15, 8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  products <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P2", "P3", "P5"),   name = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo", "Doohickey"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(orders, products, by = "product_id") report$expected_rows #> $inner #> [1] 4 #>  #> $left #> [1] 5 #>  #> $right #> [1] 5 #>  #> $full #> [1] 6 summary(report) #>               metric value #> 1          left_rows  5.00 #> 2         right_rows  4.00 #> 3   left_unique_keys  4.00 #> 4  right_unique_keys  4.00 #> 5       keys_matched  3.00 #> 6     keys_left_only  1.00 #> 7    keys_right_only  1.00 #> 8         match_rate  0.75 #> 9             issues  1.00 #> 10   inner_join_rows  4.00 #> 11    left_join_rows  5.00 #> 12   right_join_rows  5.00 #> 13    full_join_rows  6.00

title: Quick Start
text: join_explain() takes result, two input tables, join column, tells row count changed: output breaks row count contributions matched keys, unmatched keys, duplicate-driven expansion. Specifically, get: number keys matched tables; many rows matched key contributed (accounting duplicates); keys table match; net effect row count compared left table. decomposition makes straightforward answer questions like “table grow 200 rows?”, usually answer handful keys unexpected duplicates one side. omit type, joinspy infers join type row counts (though providing explicitly reliable).
code: tickets <- data.frame(   event_id = c(1, 2, 2, 3),   seat = c("A1", "B2", "B3", "C1"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  events <- data.frame(   event_id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Concert", "Play", "Opera"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  result <- merge(tickets, events, by = "event_id") join_explain(result, tickets, events, by = "event_id", type = "inner") #>  #> ── Join Explanation ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Row Counts ── #>  #> Left table (x): 4 rows #> Right table (y): 3 rows #> Result: 3 rows #> ℹ Result has 1 fewer rows than left table #>  #> ── Why the row count changed ── #>  #> ℹ Left table has 1 duplicate key(s) - each match creates multiple rows #> ℹ Inner join dropped 1 unmatched left rows

title: Quick Start
text: join_diff() compares data frame join show rows added, dropped, preserved: Row 1 dropped (match right table non-full join irrelevant since used = TRUE; row 5 added right table). kind accounting tedious hand large data. second example highlights row addition duplicates rather new keys: row count grew three four id = 1 matched two rows lookup. new keys introduced; growth came entirely duplicate-driven expansion. join_diff() distinguishes two sources row addition (new keys vs. duplicate expansion), key information need debugging unexpectedly large result.
code: before <- data.frame(   id = 1:4,   value = c("a", "b", "c", "d"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  lookup <- data.frame(   id = c(2, 3, 4, 5),   extra = c("X", "Y", "Z", "W"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  after <- merge(before, lookup, by = "id", all = TRUE) join_diff(before, after, by = "id") #>  #> ── Join Diff ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dimensions ── #>  #> Before: 4 rows x 2 columns #> After: 5 rows x 3 columns #> Change: "+1" rows, "+1" columns #>  #> ── Column Changes ── #>  #> ✔ Added: extra #>  #> ── Key Analysis ── #>  #> Unique keys before: 4 #> Unique keys after: 5 before2 <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   value = c("a", "b", "c"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  dup_lookup <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 1, 2, 3),   tag = c("X", "Y", "Z", "W"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  after2 <- merge(before2, dup_lookup, by = "id") join_diff(before2, after2, by = "id") #>  #> ── Join Diff ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dimensions ── #>  #> Before: 3 rows x 2 columns #> After: 4 rows x 3 columns #> Change: "+1" rows, "+1" columns #>  #> ── Column Changes ── #>  #> ✔ Added: tag #>  #> ── Key Analysis ── #>  #> Unique keys before: 3 #> Unique keys after: 3 #> Duplicate rows: "+2"

title: Quick Start
text: *_join_spy() family wraps standard joins automatic diagnostics. print report joining return joined data frame: four wrappers (left_join_spy, right_join_spy, inner_join_spy, full_join_spy) share interface. use wrapper instead calling join_spy() manually? wrappers convenient exploratory work interactive sessions want diagnostics printed side effect without breaking pipeline. production scripts, may prefer calling join_spy() first, inspecting report programmatically (checking report$issues report$expected_rows), proceeding join diagnostics pass. wrappers also accept backend parameter ("base", "dplyr", "data.table") overrides automatic backend detection; useful want force specific join engine regardless input class.
code: patients <- data.frame(   patient_id = c("P01", "P02", "P03"),   age = c(34, 28, 45),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  labs <- data.frame(   patient_id = c("P01", "P02", "P04"),   result = c(5.2, 3.8, 6.1),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  result <- left_join_spy(patients, labs, by = "patient_id") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: patient_id #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 2 #> Keys only in left: 1 #> Keys only in right: 1 #> Match rate (left): "66.7%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ℹ 3 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'P03' ~ 'P01', 'P03' ~ 'P02', 'P03' ~ 'P04') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 2 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 4 #> ℹ Performing "left" join... #> ✔ Result: 3 rows (as expected) head(result) #>   patient_id age result #> 1        P01  34    5.2 #> 2        P02  28    3.8 #> 3        P03  45     NA

title: Quick Start
text: pipelines, printed output distracting. Use .quiet = TRUE suppress report, retrieve afterward last_report(): last_report() always returns report recent *_join_spy() call, regardless wrapper used. report object stored internal environment persists duration R session, can inspect point join – even several lines pipeline code executed.
code: result <- inner_join_spy(patients, labs, by = "patient_id", .quiet = TRUE)  # Later, inspect what happened rpt <- last_report() rpt$match_analysis$match_rate #> [1] 0.6666667

title: Quick Start
text: join_strict() performs join throws error key relationship violates expectation. useful guard production scripts surprise many--many relationship corrupt downstream results. expect argument accepts four cardinality levels, appropriate different scenarios: "1:1": sides unique keys. Use merging two reference tables (e.g., country table currency table, keyed country code). duplicate either side error. "1:m": left table unique keys, right may repeat. classic lookup-table join: product catalogue (left) joined transactions table (right) product appears many transactions. "m:1": mirror "1:m". right table lookup; left table repeats. "m:m": duplicates allowed sides. disables constraint entirely rarely want; exists explicit opt-rare cases Cartesian product per key intentional (e.g., generating pairings two sets attributes). detect actual cardinality without enforcing anything: returns string like "1:m" can use programmatically. might use script select right expect value join_strict() dynamically, log cardinality join monitoring purposes.
code: sensors <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("T1", "T2", "T3"),   location = c("Roof", "Basement", "Lobby"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  readings <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("T1", "T2", "T3"),   value = c(22.1, 18.5, 21.0),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Succeeds: one reading per sensor join_strict(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", expect = "1:1") #>   sensor_id location value #> 1        T1     Roof  22.1 #> 2        T2 Basement  18.5 #> 3        T3    Lobby  21.0 # Fails: T1 has two readings, violating 1:1 readings_dup <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("T1", "T1", "T2", "T3"),   value = c(22.1, 23.4, 18.5, 21.0),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_strict(sensors, readings_dup, by = "sensor_id", expect = "1:1") #> Error: #> ! Cardinality violation: expected 1:1 but found 1:m #>   Left duplicates: 0, Right duplicates: 1 detect_cardinality(sensors, readings_dup, by = "sensor_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "1:m" #> Right duplicates: 1 key(s)

title: Quick Start

title: Quick Start
text: analyze_join_chain() runs diagnostics across sequence joins. provide named list tables list join specifications: step prints diagnostic report. special name "result" second step refers output previous join, can chain many tables needed. placeholder resolved internally: first join produces intermediate table, "result" swapped left (right) table next step. means key diagnostics step two operate actual intermediate data, original input, important first join may introduced NAs changed key distribution.
code: orders <- data.frame(   order_id = 1:4,   customer_id = c(1, 2, 2, 3),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = 1:3,   region_id = c(1, 1, 2),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  regions <- data.frame(   region_id = 1:2,   name = c("North", "South"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  analyze_join_chain(   tables = list(orders = orders, customers = customers, regions = regions),   joins = list(     list(left = "orders", right = "customers", by = "customer_id"),     list(left = "result", right = "regions", by = "region_id")   ) ) #>  #> ── Join Chain Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Step 1: orders + customers ── #>  #> Left: 4 rows #> Right: 3 rows #> Match rate: 100% #> Expected result: 4 rows (left join) #> ! 1 issue(s) detected #>  #> ── Step 2: result + regions ── #>  #> Left: 4 rows #> Right: 2 rows #> Match rate: 100% #> Expected result: 4 rows (left join) #> ! 1 issue(s) detected #>  #> ── Chain Summary ── #>  #> ! Total issues across chain: 2

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: small retail analytics team receives monthly sales data distribution partner. partner maintains Excel workbook; every month export CSV email . team joins internal customer database using shared customer_id column. two quarters, everything works. pipeline runs, row counts check , nobody thinks twice join. one month, 30% sales records stop matching. Nobody changed join code. Nobody changed customer database. partner swears IDs correct (, sense every ID CSV exists customer database). Eyeballing CSV confirms values look right. analyst opens R, loads tables, tries merge(). result fewer rows expected. runs setdiff() key columns sees IDs like "CUST-1002 " partner data appear internal database. Visually, looks identical "CUST-1002". trailing space , console render distinctly. data looks like: Nothing str() reveals issue. Printing data frame show either, trailing spaces invisible console output. join_spy() catches immediately: Three six partner IDs carry whitespace (leading space, trailing spaces, ). distinct character values far R concerned; "CUST-1002 " never match "CUST-1002". fix one call. join_repair() trims whitespace tables : can verify repair worked: now join gives us expected: six partner sales matched. root cause turned formula partner’s Excel sheet concatenated values two columns; one columns variable-width entries padded spaces, CONCATENATE formula preserved every byte. Nobody noticed Excel renders "CUST-1002" "CUST-1002 " exactly way. cell just shows text, trailing whitespace invisible proportional font. single common join failure seen. accounts broken pipelines string issues combined, easiest fix know .
code: partner_sales <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("CUST-1001", "CUST-1002 ", "CUST-1003",                   " CUST-1004", "CUST-1005 ", "CUST-1006"),   amount = c(2500, 1800, 3200, 950, 4100, 1600),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  internal_db <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("CUST-1001", "CUST-1002", "CUST-1003",                   "CUST-1004", "CUST-1005", "CUST-1006", "CUST-1007"),   region = c("West", "East", "West", "South", "East", "North", "West"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) report <- join_spy(partner_sales, internal_db, by = "customer_id") repaired <- join_repair(partner_sales, internal_db, by = "customer_id") #> ✔ Repaired 3 value(s) partner_fixed <- repaired$x internal_fixed <- repaired$y key_check(partner_fixed, internal_fixed, by = "customer_id") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected result <- merge(partner_fixed, internal_fixed, by = "customer_id") nrow(result) #> [1] 6

CRM stores email addresses uppercase (decision database migration late 1990s nobody revisited). web app’s event log stores lowercase, browsers submit. systems running independently years; team wants join CRM profiles clickstream events see subscription plans correlate engagement. team writes simple inner join gets zero rows back. Zero. “fewer expected”: literally nothing. first suspect data load correctly, tables right number rows columns . problem simpler frustrating: R’s string comparison case-sensitive, "ALICE@ACME.COM" equal "alice@acme.com". Every single key pair fails match, even though underlying email addresses obviously . join_spy() flags situation join even happens: case mismatch issue appears report. suggest_repairs() can generate specific code fix : can use join_repair() directly, specifying case standardization: lowercasing sides, inner join returns four matched rows (everyone except Eve, click data, Frank, CRM): interesting thing scenario neither data source “wrong.” internally consistent; problem exists boundary . common shape join failures organizational data work: system correct isolation, maintained different team, documented way. incompatibility surfaces someone tries connect , person connecting usually person chose casing convention either system. Note email addresses case-insensitive RFC 5321 (local part @ technically can case-sensitive, almost mail server enforces practice). Lowercasing right normalization . types identifiers (product codes, country abbreviations, ticker symbols) may want "upper" instead. point pick one convention apply sides joining.
code: crm_profiles <- data.frame(   email = c("ALICE@ACME.COM", "BOB@ACME.COM", "CAROL@ACME.COM",             "DAVE@ACME.COM", "EVE@ACME.COM"),   plan = c("enterprise", "starter", "pro", "enterprise", "starter"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  click_events <- data.frame(   email = c("alice@acme.com", "bob@acme.com", "carol@acme.com",             "dave@acme.com", "frank@acme.com"),   page_views = c(47, 12, 89, 33, 5),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) report <- join_spy(crm_profiles, click_events, by = "email") suggest_repairs(report) #>  #> ── Suggested Repairs ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> # Standardize case: #> x$email <- tolower(x$email) #> y$email <- tolower(y$email) repaired <- join_repair(   crm_profiles, click_events,   by = "email",   standardize_case = "lower" ) #> ✔ Repaired 5 value(s) result <- merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "email") nrow(result) #> [1] 4 result #>            email       plan page_views #> 1 alice@acme.com enterprise         47 #> 2   bob@acme.com    starter         12 #> 3 carol@acme.com        pro         89 #> 4  dave@acme.com enterprise         33

Every country name spelled correctly prints data frame. columns right types. joins reference table population data, half countries fail match. checks typos manually; none. runs unique() country columns compares eye; look identical. Watch happens print PDF data: country names look perfectly clean. zero-width space “India” “France” literally invisible; occupies zero pixels width. non-breaking space “Japan” renders identically regular space (trimws() catch), Unicode character U+00A0, U+0020. plain trimws() call remove R versions. merge confirms damage: three six countries match. researcher might spend hour checking typos exist, characters causing failure invisible human eye. join_spy() detects encoding issues string inspection : report flags invisible characters left table. Repair straightforward: Six matches, expected. zero-width spaces non-breaking spaces gone. class problem hard catch. Every standard debugging tool (print(), str(), summary(), unique(), even visual comparison spreadsheet) show data looks correct. can copy value console, paste string comparison, fail, understand . characters byte representation; simply visible glyph. PDF copy-paste common source, web scraping, OCR output, data legacy mainframe systems can produce artifacts. One useful debugging trick outside joinspy: suspect invisible characters, run nchar() values. "India\u200B" 6 characters, 5. requires already suspect problem exists. join_spy() flags proactively, spent hour staring identical-looking strings.
code: # Simulating PDF copy-paste artifacts: # \u00A0 is non-breaking space, \u200B is zero-width space pdf_data <- data.frame(   country = c("Brazil", "India\u200B", "Germany",               "Japan\u00A0", "Canada", "France\u200B"),   prevalence = c(12.3, 8.7, 5.1, 3.9, 6.2, 4.8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  reference <- data.frame(   country = c("Brazil", "India", "Germany", "Japan",               "Canada", "France", "Italy"),   population_m = c(214, 1408, 84, 125, 38, 68, 59),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) pdf_data$country #> [1] "Brazil"  "India​"   "Germany" "Japan "  "Canada"  "France​" nrow(merge(pdf_data, reference, by = "country")) #> [1] 3 report <- join_spy(pdf_data, reference, by = "country") repaired <- join_repair(pdf_data, reference, by = "country") #> ✔ Repaired 3 value(s) nrow(merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "country")) #> [1] 6

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: Consider monthly ETL pipeline stable year e-commerce company. Transaction records point--sale system joined product catalogue attach product names, categories, profit margins. Finance uses output monthly reporting. pipeline ran cleanly months. match rate started drifting downward: 99% January, 97% February, 94% March. pipeline fail; just returned slightly fewer rows month. Nobody noticed April, finance flagged discrepancy margin totals. code changed. product catalogue changed. changed person. new data entry clerk joined warehouse team December. existing convention product codes strict: "WDG-100", "WDG-101", uppercase prefix, dash, three-digit numeric suffix. new clerk sometimes omitted dash, sometimes typed lowercase, sometimes . codes close enough warehouse system accepted (fuzzy matching internally), join ETL pipeline . match; . damage partial, makes harder notice complete failure. join_spy() shows picture: report flags case mismatches unmatched keys. scenario differs previous ones: join_repair() can fix case issue, fix missing dashes. Whitespace trimming case standardization mechanical transformations; inserting dash "WDG102" make match "WDG-102" requires knowledge formatting rule, tool . can dry run see join_repair() fix: applying mechanical fixes: case issues resolved, dash-versus--dash mismatches remain. require manual transformation: Now can verify: eight transactions match. scenario worth sitting moment, illustrates boundary automated repair can . join_repair() handles mechanical transformations: trimming whitespace, normalizing case, stripping invisible characters. context-free operations always safe apply. inserting dash "WDG102" produce "WDG-102" requires knowing canonical format uses dash three letters. domain knowledge. join_spy() show keys unmatched flag near-matches, fix come someone understands data. practice, right response kind drift just regex pipeline; conversation warehouse team enforcing code format point entry. pipeline fix bandage; process fix cure.
code: # Product catalogue (canonical format) catalogue <- data.frame(   product_code = c("WDG-100", "WDG-101", "WDG-102",                    "WDG-103", "WDG-104", "WDG-105"),   product_name = c("Widget A", "Widget B", "Widget C",                    "Widget D", "Widget E", "Widget F"),   margin = c(0.35, 0.28, 0.42, 0.31, 0.39, 0.25),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Recent transactions (mix of old and new clerk entries) transactions <- data.frame(   product_code = c("WDG-100", "WDG-101", "WDG102",                    "wdg-103", "WDG-104", "wdg105",                    "WDG-100", "WDG103"),   quantity = c(5, 3, 7, 2, 4, 6, 1, 8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) report <- join_spy(transactions, catalogue, by = "product_code") join_repair(transactions, catalogue,             by = "product_code",             standardize_case = "upper",             dry_run = TRUE) #>  #> ── Repair Preview (Dry Run) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Left table (x) ── #>  #> ℹ product_code: upper case (2) repaired <- join_repair(transactions, catalogue,                         by = "product_code",                         standardize_case = "upper") #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) # Manual fix: insert dash if missing in product codes matching the pattern fix_codes <- function(codes) {   gsub("^([A-Z]{3})(\\d)", "\\1-\\2", codes) } repaired$x$product_code <- fix_codes(repaired$x$product_code) result <- merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "product_code") nrow(result) #> [1] 8

title: Strict Join with Cardinality Enforcement — join_strict
text: x data frame (left table). y data frame (right table). character vector column names join . type Character. type join perform. One "left" (default), "right", "inner", "full". expect Character. expected cardinality relationship. One : "1:1" key x matches one key y, vice versa "1:m" "1:many" key x can match multiple keys y, key y matches one key x "m:1" "many:1" key y can match multiple keys x, key x matches one key y "m:m" "many:many" cardinality constraints (allows relationships) backend Character NULL. join backend use. NULL (default), auto-detects input class. See left_join_spy() details. ... Additional arguments passed underlying join function.

code: orders <- data.frame(id = 1:3, product = c("A", "B", "C")) customers <- data.frame(id = 1:3, name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol"))  # This succeeds (1:1 relationship) join_strict(orders, customers, by = "id", expect = "1:1")  # This fails if customers had duplicate ids (wrapped in try to show error) customers_dup <- data.frame(id = c(1, 1, 2), name = c("A1", "A2", "B")) try(join_strict(orders, customers_dup, by = "id", expect = "1:1"))

title: Log Join Report to File — log_report
text: report JoinReport object join_spy() retrieved via last_report(). file File path write . Extension determines format: .txt .log: Plain text (human-readable) .json: JSON format (machine-readable) .rds: R binary format (preserves data) append Logical.
